column1: What proportion of the human genome encodes proteins?
column2: About 1.5%. Only a small fraction of the genome is protein-coding; much disease-related regulation sits in noncoding DNA. [1][2]

column1: What type of DNA element can increase transcription from a distance?
column2: An enhancer. Enhancers are noncoding regulatory elements that can increase transcription from a distance. [2]

column1: What term describes large duplicated, deleted, or rearranged DNA segments?
column2: Copy number variation (CNV). These are large contiguous genomic gains, losses, or rearrangements. [2][3]

column1: What term describes coinheritance of nearby variants because of physical proximity?
column2: Linkage disequilibrium. Nearby variants tend to be inherited together because they sit close together on the chromosome. [2][3]

column1: What does epigenetics refer to?
column2: Heritable change in gene expression without an altered DNA sequence. This explains different phenotypes from the same genome. [3]

column1: What is the usual effect of DNA methylation at regulatory regions?
column2: Chromatin condensation and transcriptional silencing. DNA methylation typically represses transcription at regulatory regions. [4]

column1: What is the usual effect of histone acetylation?
column2: Open chromatin with increased transcription. Histone acetylation generally makes DNA more transcriptionally accessible. [3][4]

column1: What is the main action of microRNAs?
column2: Post-transcriptional gene silencing. microRNAs act through RISC to cleave target mRNA or repress translation. [4][5]

column1: What is XIST?
column2: A long noncoding RNA that mediates X-chromosome inactivation. [4][5]

column1: Which DNA repair pathway enables precise CRISPR correction when donor DNA is supplied?
column2: Homology-directed repair (HDR). This is the precise pathway used when donor DNA is available. [5][6]

column1: Which repair pathway is more error-prone after a double-strand DNA break: NHEJ or HDR?
column2: Nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ). It commonly introduces insertions or deletions after double-strand breaks. [5][6]

column1: Which membrane phospholipid flips outward during apoptosis as an "eat me" signal?
column2: Phosphatidylserine. It normally sits on the inner leaflet and flips outward during apoptosis. [8]

column1: What is the immediate membrane-transport consequence of ATP depletion?
column2: Failure of ion pumps with osmotic cell swelling. ATP depletion prevents the cell from pumping out inorganic ions. [9]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cellular-housekeeping atp-depletion cell-swelling high-yield

column1: Which process internalizes LDL through specific surface receptors?
column2: Clathrin-mediated receptor-mediated endocytosis. LDL uptake is the classic example. [10][11]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cellular-housekeeping endocytosis ldl clathrin

column1: What do tight junctions primarily maintain?
column2: Paracellular barrier formation and apical-basolateral polarity. [12]

column1: What do gap junctions allow to pass between adjacent cells?
column2: Ions and small molecules. Gap junctions help coordinate tissues such as cardiac muscle. [12]

column1: Cytokeratin expression points to what cell lineage?
column2: Epithelial lineage. Cytokeratins are epithelial intermediate filaments. [11]

column1: Which organelle synthesizes and folds membrane and secreted proteins?
column2: Rough endoplasmic reticulum. It synthesizes and folds membrane and secreted proteins. [12]

column1: Where is the common misfolded CFTR mutant retained?
column2: Rough endoplasmic reticulum. The common CFTR mutant is misfolded, retained, and degraded there. [12]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cellular-housekeeping cftr rough-er high-yield

column1: What Golgi-added signal targets enzymes to lysosomes?
column2: Mannose-6-phosphate. This Golgi-added tag directs hydrolases to lysosomes. [12][13]

column1: What is the main role of the Golgi apparatus?
column2: Protein modification, sorting, and trafficking. The Golgi sends cargo to the membrane, lysosomes, or secretory vesicles. [12][13]

column1: What degrades ubiquitin-tagged cytosolic proteins?
column2: Proteasome. It degrades ubiquitin-tagged cytosolic proteins, including short-lived regulatory proteins. [13][14]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cellular-housekeeping proteasome ubiquitin high-yield

column1: What process delivers senescent organelles to lysosomes in a double-membrane vesicle?
column2: Autophagy. Senescent organelles are enclosed in an autophagosome before lysosomal degradation. [13][14]

column1: Why is mitochondrial inheritance usually maternal?
column2: Because the ovum contributes nearly all cytoplasmic organelles to the zygote. This makes mitochondrial inheritance usually maternal. [13][14]

column1: What metabolic shift defines the Warburg effect?
column2: Increased glycolysis with lactate production despite adequate oxygen. This preserves carbon skeletons for biosynthesis. [15]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cellular-housekeeping warburg-effect metabolism high-yield

column1: Release of what mitochondrial protein promotes apoptosis?
column2: Cytochrome c. Its release from mitochondria helps trigger apoptosis. [15]

column1: Which second messenger mediates nitric oxide signaling in vascular smooth muscle?
column2: cGMP. Nitric oxide activates guanylyl cyclase in vascular smooth muscle. [16]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cellular-housekeeping nitric-oxide cgmp high-yield

column1: What activates most receptor tyrosine kinases?
column2: Ligand-induced dimerization with autophosphorylation. That is the core activation mechanism for receptor tyrosine kinases. [16][17]

column1: What is the key signaling event in Notch activation?
column2: Proteolytic receptor cleavage with nuclear translocation of the intracellular fragment. That is the hallmark of Notch signaling. [17][18]

column1: What molecule is stabilized in Wnt/Frizzled signaling?
column2: Beta-catenin. Wnt signaling stabilizes beta-catenin and allows nuclear signaling. [18]

column1: What is the effect of delayed GTP hydrolysis in mutant RAS?
column2: Persistent proliferative signaling. Delayed GTP hydrolysis keeps RAS in its active state. [17]

column1: Which growth factor is the major driver of angiogenesis?
column2: VEGF. It is the major driver of angiogenesis. [20]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cell-signaling-and-growth-factors vegf angiogenesis high-yield

column1: Which growth factor also increases vascular permeability during vessel growth?
column2: VEGF. It increases vascular permeability as well as angiogenesis. [20]

column1: Which cytokine or growth factor drives ECM synthesis and fibrosis through Smad signaling?
column2: TGF-beta. It signals through Smads to promote ECM synthesis and fibrosis. [20][21]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cell-signaling-and-growth-factors tgf-beta fibrosis high-yield

column1: Which receptor class is used by steroid hormones?
column2: Intracellular or nuclear receptors. Steroid hormones cross the membrane and regulate transcription directly. [16]

column1: What is another name for hepatocyte growth factor (HGF)?
column2: Scatter factor. HGF is both mitogenic and motogenic in epithelial repair. [19][20]

column1: What is the main action of PDGF in tissue repair?
column2: Recruitment and proliferation of fibroblasts, smooth muscle cells, and endothelial cells. PDGF is important in repair and fibrosis. [20]

column1: Which major proliferative pathways are activated downstream of RAS?
column2: MAPK and PI3K/Akt/mTOR. These are the major proliferative pathways downstream of RAS. [17]
column3: pathology chapter-01 cell-signaling-and-growth-factors ras mapk pi3k

column1: Which extracellular matrix structure lies beneath epithelium, endothelium, and smooth muscle?
column2: Basement membrane. It is the organized ECM layer beneath those cell types. [22]

column1: What is the major adhesive glycoprotein of basement membrane?
column2: Laminin. It is the dominant adhesive glycoprotein of basement membrane. [24]

column1: What is the main collagenous scaffold of basement membrane?
column2: Type IV collagen. It is the characteristic collagen of basement membrane. [22]

column1: What major role does fibronectin play in wound healing?
column2: It provides a scaffold for ECM deposition and re-epithelialization. That is its key role in wound healing. [24]

column1: Defect in which extracellular matrix component causes Marfan syndrome?
column2: Fibrillin. Defect of fibrillin causes Marfan syndrome. [23]

column1: What peptide motif is recognized by many integrins?
column2: RGD. Many integrins recognize this peptide motif on ECM proteins such as fibronectin. [24]
column3: pathology chapter-01 extracellular-matrix integrin rgd high-yield

column1: What do integrins physically link to the extracellular matrix?
column2: The intracellular cytoskeleton. Integrins connect ECM outside the cell to cytoskeletal structures inside the cell. [24]

column1: What is the main function of proteoglycans in extracellular matrix?
column2: They create a hydrated gel and reservoir for growth factors. [23]

column1: What is the restriction point of the cell cycle?
column2: The late G1 commitment point. After the restriction point, the cell no longer needs ongoing growth factor stimulation to continue the cycle. [24][25]

column1: In which phase of the cell cycle does DNA synthesis occur?
column2: S phase. That is the phase of DNA synthesis. [25]

column1: What drives the G1 to S transition?
column2: Cyclin D-CDK4/6 and cyclin E-CDK2 phosphorylating Rb. This drives the G1 to S transition. [25][26]

column1: Which complex is essential for the G2 to M transition?
column2: Cyclin B-CDK1. It is essential for the G2 to M transition. [25][26]

column1: What do p21, p27, and p57 do?
column2: They inhibit multiple CDKs. The Cip/Kip family restrains cell-cycle progression. [25][26]

column1: What are the two defining properties of stem cells?
column2: Self-renewal and generation of differentiated progeny. These two features define a stem cell. [26]

column1: What do adult stem-cell niches mainly do?
column2: They keep stem cells quiescent and regulate activation when expansion is needed. [27][28]

column1: What is an induced pluripotent stem cell?
column2: A reprogrammed somatic cell with embryonic stem cell-like pluripotency. [28]
